name: Food & Snacks
description: Solid food items and snacks are generally allowed in both carry-on and checked baggage. Liquid, gel, or spreadable foods in carry-on must follow the 3-1-1 liquids rule (3.4oz/100ml containers in a quart bag).
tsaGuideline: Solid food items are allowed in both carry-on and checked bags. Foods that are liquid, gel, paste, or spreadable (soup, sauces, hummus, peanut butter, yogurt) must comply with the 3-1-1 rule in carry-on: containers of 3.4oz or less in a quart-sized bag. TSA may ask you to separate food from your bag for X-ray screening.
iataGuideline: IATA defers food restrictions to national aviation authorities. International travelers should be aware that many countries have strict customs regulations on importing food products, particularly meat, dairy, fruits, and vegetables.
restrictions: Liquid, gel, or spreadable foods must follow 3-1-1 rule in carry-on
TSA may request food items be placed in a separate bin for screening
International travel may have customs restrictions on importing food
Meat, fruits, and vegetables may be prohibited on international/agricultural flights
Frozen items allowed if fully frozen solid at checkpoint; partially thawed items follow liquid rules

faqs.question: Can I bring sandwiches and snacks through airport security?
faqs.answer: Yes. Solid foods like sandwiches, chips, granola bars, fruits, nuts, and candy are allowed in carry-on without restriction. You may be asked to remove them from your bag for separate X-ray screening.

faqs.question: What foods count as liquids under TSA rules?
faqs.answer: Foods that are pourable, spreadable, or gel-like count as liquids. This includes soup, yogurt, hummus, peanut butter, jelly, salsa, ice cream, creamy cheese, and sauces. In carry-on, these must be in containers of 3.4oz or less.

faqs.question: Can I bring a frozen meal through security?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it is completely frozen solid at the time of screening. If it has started to melt or is partially thawed, it will be treated as a liquid and must follow the 3-1-1 rule. Ice packs must also be completely frozen.
